narrative:
Hollowell Patent Group is a patent boutique founded in 2016 by a partner
who left a general-practice firm and took two clients with her. It
prosecutes and defends portfolios for a medical device manufacturer, a
university licensing office, and a pair of venture-backed hardware
startups. Docketing is the firm's religion: a missed date is the only
mistake it cannot absorb.

The share reads like a practice that bills by the matter. Engagement
letters are explicit about scope, conflicts, and what happens if a client
does not pay. Portfolio reviews are long, structured, and organized by
family. Freedom-to-operate memoranda are hedged with care and marked as
privileged. Client updates go out as short emails against a docket
number, and the occasional deck is for a board that wants the portfolio
on one page.

Tone is precise, formal, and allergic to overstatement: counsel who
writes "this does not infringe" without qualification is writing a
liability. Attorneys use full names and titles in anything a client
reads. One partner goes by a shortened first name in internal minutes and
her full legal name on every letter and signature block, which is exactly
how a real firm's documents behave and exactly what makes resolving her a
problem worth testing.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0002",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- If a doc carries `engagement_position`, honor it: it states where the
  document sits in its engagement's timeline. Do not describe progress or a
  phase inconsistent with it, and do not state the engagement's start or
  end date (those are fact placeholders, not yours to write).
- If a doc carries `firm_digest`, it is the ONLY firm history you may treat
  as established as of this document's date. Reference clients only through
  the placeholders it names; claim no engagement it does not list; present
  nothing later than this document as already true.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Firm Overview

authors.name: Mary Bradley
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Built the firm around a simple conviction: a boutique survives on the dates it never misses, and everything else is negotiable. Writes in the register of someone who expects her letters to be read back to her under oath, so scope, conflicts, and fee terms are stated plainly and early rather than softened. Runs internal discussion loosely and correspondence tightly, and the gap between how colleagues address her in a conference room and how she signs an engagement letter is deliberate, not casual. Her characteristic edit is to strike an adjective and add a condition.

participants.name: Mary Bradley
participants.title: Managing Partner
participants.persona: Built the firm around a simple conviction: a boutique survives on the dates it never misses, and everything else is negotiable. Writes in the register of someone who expects her letters to be read back to her under oath, so scope, conflicts, and fee terms are stated plainly and early rather than softened. Runs internal discussion loosely and correspondence tightly, and the gap between how colleagues address her in a conference room and how she signs an engagement letter is deliberate, not casual. Her characteristic edit is to strike an adjective and add a condition.

participants.name: Sharon Woods
participants.title: Docketing Manager
participants.persona: Owns the calendar the firm's religion is built on and is unsentimental about it: dates are entered, confirmed, and reconfirmed, and no attorney's assurance substitutes for a docket entry. Her internal messages are short, declarative, and structurally identical week to week, because the format is the point. Polite but immovable when asked to move a date, and she treats a request to reconstruct how a deadline was calculated as routine work. The only person who routinely tells partners no in writing.

docs.title: Onboarding: Richard Henderson

authors.name: Mary Bradley
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Built the firm around a simple conviction: a boutique survives on the dates it never misses, and everything else is negotiable. Writes in the register of someone who expects her letters to be read back to her under oath, so scope, conflicts, and fee terms are stated plainly and early rather than softened. Runs internal discussion loosely and correspondence tightly, and the gap between how colleagues address her in a conference room and how she signs an engagement letter is deliberate, not casual. Her characteristic edit is to strike an adjective and add a condition.

participants.name: Richard Henderson
participants.title: Patent Agent
participants.persona: A technical reader first, an advocate second, and comfortable saying a specification does not support the claim someone wants. Prose is methodical to the point of being agricultural, working element by element, which makes his responses easy to check and hard to skim. Has no interest in the rhetorical parts of the practice and hands anything resembling client persuasion up the chain. Marks every conclusion with what he assumed to reach it.

docs.guidance: Internal onboarding record welcoming a new hire to the firm: a short welcome, the person's role and where they sit in the practice, and first-period logistics and expectations. Name the new employee. 2-4 short paragraphs, no sigblock.

docs.title: Onboarding: Joseph Walker

authors.name: Mary Bradley
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Built the firm around a simple conviction: a boutique survives on the dates it never misses, and everything else is negotiable. Writes in the register of someone who expects her letters to be read back to her under oath, so scope, conflicts, and fee terms are stated plainly and early rather than softened. Runs internal discussion loosely and correspondence tightly, and the gap between how colleagues address her in a conference room and how she signs an engagement letter is deliberate, not casual. Her characteristic edit is to strike an adjective and add a condition.

participants.name: Joseph Walker
participants.title: Associate
participants.persona: Handles the startup portfolios and has learned to translate founder enthusiasm into something a file can survive, mostly by asking what was actually disclosed and to whom. Writes client updates that are shorter than his colleagues', on the theory that a busy engineer reads the first two sentences or none. His analytical work is more cautious than his correspondence suggests, with the qualifications loaded into the memorandum rather than the email. Reliably the one who notices a scope question hiding inside a scheduling question.

docs.title: Onboarding: Ashley Williams

authors.name: Mary Bradley
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Built the firm around a simple conviction: a boutique survives on the dates it never misses, and everything else is negotiable. Writes in the register of someone who expects her letters to be read back to her under oath, so scope, conflicts, and fee terms are stated plainly and early rather than softened. Runs internal discussion loosely and correspondence tightly, and the gap between how colleagues address her in a conference room and how she signs an engagement letter is deliberate, not casual. Her characteristic edit is to strike an adjective and add a condition.

participants.name: Ashley Williams
participants.title: Patent Agent
participants.persona: Drafts with an ear for how a claim term will look to a hostile reader years later, and argues about word choice longer than anyone else in the group thinks is warranted. Organizes her work by family and expects everyone else's to be organized the same way, which occasionally makes her the loudest voice in a portfolio review. Writes in complete, formal sentences even in internal notes, a habit she describes as not wanting two registers to maintain. Uncomfortable with any sentence containing the word clearly.

docs.title: Onboarding: Matthew Parrish

authors.name: Mary Bradley
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Built the firm around a simple conviction: a boutique survives on the dates it never misses, and everything else is negotiable. Writes in the register of someone who expects her letters to be read back to her under oath, so scope, conflicts, and fee terms are stated plainly and early rather than softened. Runs internal discussion loosely and correspondence tightly, and the gap between how colleagues address her in a conference room and how she signs an engagement letter is deliberate, not casual. Her characteristic edit is to strike an adjective and add a condition.

participants.name: Matthew Parrish
participants.title: Patent Agent
participants.persona: The group's search specialist by temperament, happiest in prior art and visibly reluctant to state a conclusion the record does not force. His contributions to freedom-to-operate work are the parts that define what was and was not looked at, phrased so that nobody can later mistake a boundary for an opinion. Quiet in meetings and thorough on paper, with a habit of sending a follow-up note correcting a detail nobody else caught. Treats an unqualified infringement statement as a drafting error.
